english_query: counterexample to: Two sets A and B have the same cardinality if and only if (i) there exists a function f from A to B, (ii) f is injective, and (iii) f is surjective.
ugf_response: First, we look at what happens when two people want different things. One person wants to be nice, and the other wants to take a big part of the money for themselves. If they both try to take a small share, they fight. This hurts both of them. But if they pick to take a little bit each, the other might take it all later. Then both lose out. This is the worst thing that can happen. Next, we think about trust. You cannot just throw things away. You must know that the other person will do their part. If you act like you are the only one who holds the land, you are not ready for it. You might worry they will hit you or take your stuff, so they stay back. If both act this way, no one wins. If one picks to be hard and kind while the other is soft, the mean one gets the most gold and the soft one gets nothing. This shows us how working together needs trust. Some might say we should just talk and agree on a plan. But talking does not make sure the other person is good at thinking. Without trust, fear stops us from doing the best thing. So, the best answer is that we need a clear promise or a strong way to watch the work. Without that promise, we just go with the bad move because we could have had a better ending. So my answer is: We need a way to make promises or a strong promise, like being nice and getting help, to win.

english_query: counterexample to: A region R is a space if and only if (i) R is bounded — that is, R has limits or borders that distinguish it from what lies outside, (ii) R is capable of containing material objects, and (iii) the positions of objects within R can be specified by a finite set of numerical coordinates.
ugf_response: First, we see that doing things in a way that fits our own words makes us strong. This means the other side sees that we are true to who we are. Then, when they find out our old thoughts were wrong, we change how we see the truth. We look at our own mind and let it lead us away from the right path. Some might say that just changing words does not fix the real problem, but ignoring the bad parts is still a choice that gives no good reason. So, these three ways help keep our thinking clear and close to what is really happening. So my answer is: The five ways show that we change from being wrong because they hide our true beliefs.

english_query: counterexample to: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A consists of a set of premisses and a conclusion, (ii) the conclusion follows from the premisses by the rules of a recognised formal system, and (iii) each step in the derivation of the conclusion is sanctioned by an explicitly stated rule of inference.
ugf_response: First we see that a person who makes things and puts a made group of possible kinds of things on a board. Then we note that if we pick a small group that is not good or not in order they can be taken from the group and get a not good thing. So they need to change the way they put together what they like to make sure the whole group stays good. Next we think about what could go wrong if we do not pick them which might stop the whole group from being good or bad. One strong answer is to try to look at all groups at once because that gives the most true good thing no matter how we add up the group. Another view is that we cannot trust any order because the order may just be in the group, not in the truth of the numbers. Even so, using an order where it comes from does not fix the problem of using a not good end instead of a better one. So we should always pick the group that makes the big whole group stay good. So my answer is: The best plan is to use an order that only uses a not good final end, not just the order itself.

english_query: counterexample to: A proposition P is a tautology if and only if (i) P is true in all possible circumstances, (ii) P's truth can be established by examining its logical form alone, without knowledge of the truth-values of its components, and (iii) P is expressible using only the standard truth-functional connectives.
ugf_response: First, we look at a way to pick what someone says. This helps when there are many choices and no one way is best for everyone. It also works well when people do not know everything about each other. Then, we think about why this matters. If you make a choice because you cannot see everything, or because your mind plays games with you, it hurts you. The fact that your choice does not change the world means your choice did not help you get what you want. So, the way of picking only things that follow what you can see and count leads to bad picks, which can lead to wrong moves. But someone might say that even if this is hard, you still need ways to choose to avoid trouble, like making sure your choice fits your life. They might worry that a small thing done is better than big ones later. However, the question asks us to look at the shape of the choice. The shape shows how the way of choosing breaks the tie between what is known and what you want to find out. This shows that the way of thinking must work for every choice from the start. So my answer is: The way of showing how a person who thinks well should care about how their choice changes the lives of others, not just about whether the choice is good or bad.

english_query: counterexample to: A political entity E is sovereign if and only if (i) E exercises supreme authority within a defined territory — no higher authority overrides its decisions, (ii) E controls the use of force within that territory, and (iii) E is recognised as sovereign by other sovereign entities.
ugf_response: First, we look at a way to make choices using chances. This helps when things do not change. We can call this chance the first thing and the second thing. Then we add them all together for each choice to see what happens most often. This shows how good the end is when everyone uses the same way. Next, we check if a way changes when it gets more or less. If it does, it cannot do this alone because it must always give more or less than the others. So we find that the numbers we get are tied to where they are in the world, making the adding up safe from changing how people act over time. Someone might say that changing your mind is hard or confusing, but if the counting says one way is better in every case, you have to think about which wrong part matters most. So my answer is: The counting way explains how good the last step is because it needs those who change their ways to be the same as the group, but a changing group can happen even when people try to take extra from it.

english_query: counterexample to: A person P acquires title to land by prescription if and only if (i) P has been in continuous and uninterrupted possession of the land for the requisite statutory period, (ii) P's possession has been open and notorious — that is, visible to the world and not concealed, and (iii) P's possession has been adverse to the interests of the true owner — that is, without the true owner's permission.
ugf_response: First we notice that people say they have seen the person own idea and feel it is about them. Then this can make them think that because they are good at school they will see more people who do work and may give ideas that are more kind. But someone could say maybe the person does not really know enough or that the new plan is too hard. Even so the many times show that the story is not strong. So my answer is: It is most probably that a person who does not really understand can think they know a lot but also can be careful and not trust their old idea alone.

english_query: counterexample to: A person P perceives an object O if and only if (i) P has a sensory experience with phenomenal content that represents O, (ii) P can attend to O and discriminate O from its surroundings, and (iii) P can identify O — that is, P can say what O is.
ugf_response: First, we look at the idea of doing what others do. This means we cannot just pick one side and stick to it without looking hard. Then, we see how this helps us make good choices. When people try to sell or help each other, they often need to know who gets what. But we must also think about the bad parts. If no one knows who gets what, they might stop trying. So, the way things work says we must use these facts for every choice. This makes the big picture clear because no one has to guess if the other person will change their mind later. Someone might say this is too wild because it takes too long. But even then, we should not forget to add up the past choices. We check the numbers to be sure. This helps us avoid bad spots. So my answer is: The best way to fix this is to use facts about how people act now to show us the path ahead, rather than guessing without showing why.

english_query: counterexample to: A person S is entitled to restitution from a person T if and only if (i) T has been unjustly enriched at S's expense, (ii) there is no legal justification for T's retention of the enrichment, and (iii) S has suffered a corresponding loss.
ugf_response: First we see that the first person shows a paper that says he gets money when he does a good thing and a paper that say he must do it when he does not. Then we note that she does not have to pay any money or say no. Next we look at what we care about: she feels the work they did was right and can keep going even if the paper was not clear. This means she can get more money by doing a good thing while also feeling a bad feeling because she cannot see how the paper works now. Some might say that she could still do a good thing but her feeling may be built on fear of being called bad and that other things like chance or outside force could make her keep the paper. So the best answer is that she should keep working with the paper to show that the work is good and the paper should not hide that. So my answer is: The first person is wrong to think the work is fine just because she feels the job is right and her pain comes from doing a good act.

english_query: counterexample to: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, (ii) all atoms of S have the same number of protons, and (iii) S occurs naturally on Earth.
ugf_response: First, we look at the idea that a thing works just because it is good for one person but bad for another. This is not right because people need different things like food and safe places. Next, we think about what happens when someone says a thing works only because you get more stuff than others, even if you do not get more stuff. This makes other people stop trusting the idea because they feel it does not help them as much. Then, we consider how to fix this. We can say that any thing has a way to share the money or good things with others, so it helps us reach the best end point. We use a clear path where every thing gets the same share. So, the way we talk about the real good is by making sure no one can change their mind if they see that the shared plan gives them more. Finally, we check if there are any times when it might be wrong to let go of that one thing. But usually, these two ways keep the idea in place. So my answer is: a thing works best when there are many good things that everyone can use, not just for itself, because it stops giving the same share to each person without taking away good things from another.

english_query: counterexample to: An ordered field F is complete if and only if (i) F is Archimedean — that is, for every element x of F there exists a natural number n such that n > x, (ii) F contains the rational numbers as a subfield, and (iii) every element of F can be expressed as a ratio of two integers.
ugf_response: First we see that when people meet many times they may need to pick each thing by chance so the count gets bigger each time they do this kind of work together. Then we note that the count of the usual way of picking things changes over time so what was good before may not be good now. Some might say that using a simple idea like always pick the best thing but that may stop us from changing what really works and could cause a bad thing later. Even so we should also think about how much money each person needs and how much they trust each other, because if we ignore those we may lose the good end. So the safe plan is to use a simple way of choosing each thing while still looking at the usual way each time, not just the most liked one, and to watch for any wrong parts in the counting. So my answer is: The safe plan follows the normal way of picking each thing, while changing it until one of them fails, though we should watch for the usual way.

english_query: counterexample to: A person possesses intellectual humility if and only if (i) the person has an accurate assessment of the limits of the person's own knowledge and cognitive abilities, (ii) the person does not claim greater epistemic authority than is warranted by the person's actual competence, and (iii) the person is disposed to acknowledge the person's own errors and to credit the contributions of others.

Next we think about why this matters because the person can be made to do something not true, can lose trust from others, and can get into a fight that hurts many. So the best answer is that this kind of seeing makes the idea of being same and careful better for the person and helps the group stay safe. So my answer is: So the idea is right that showing a person is more important helps them stay together and helps the plan work better.

english_query: counterexample to: A narrative N constitutes a legitimate historical narrative if and only if (i) N recounts events in their correct temporal sequence, (ii) N is grounded in authentic primary sources, and (iii) N provides a coherent account of why events occurred as they did.

Next the person has another idea that says nothing bad will happen unless the person already thinks the thing was a big problem. This shows a mind way where we like safe things more than a small chance. But someone could say maybe the person really does want to do something even though it did not cause a hurt. Even so the person still looks for real signs and wants to stop the hurt if they can. So my answer is: The person likes safe things more because they want a good end, even though it caused a hurt, and wants to avoid a hurt.

english_query: counterexample to: A region R is a space if and only if (i) R is bounded — that is, R has limits or borders that distinguish it from what lies outside, (ii) R is capable of containing material objects, and (iii) the positions of objects within R can be specified by a finite set of numerical coordinates.

So my answer is: The most strong answer is that if you do not trust a paper you may put your hope on a lie and give you nothing good.

english_query: counterexample to: A behaviour B is instinctive if and only if (i) B is exhibited by all normal members of the species, (ii) B appears without prior learning or training, and (iii) B is performed in a fixed, stereotyped pattern that does not vary across individuals or occasions.
ugf_response: First, we look at the idea that picking a way to act helps if everyone uses it at the same time. This seems good because we cannot change things once we are there. If you pick a path and everyone knows what happens next, the first choice gives you more power than the second. Second, we think about why people do not change their ways when they see others doing well. They know others will also pick the best way for them. This makes sense because we need a clear plan that works every time. Third, someone might say that just knowing is enough. But without knowing the reason, we might be wrong about what will happen. The strongest answer is that knowing the reason leads us to the right pick without any hard thinking. So my answer is: Choosing a way to live helps us reach the best thing, which is a strong step from learning how people really work.

english_query: counterexample to: An object A at time t1 is identical with an object B at time t2 if and only if (i) A and B are composed of the same material parts, (ii) A and B have the same form or structure — that is, their parts are arranged in the same way, and (iii) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting A at t1 with B at t2 along which no disruption of existence occurs.

Next we think about a place where people watch shows. In that place some say the chance idea can make the player act in another way so the idea matters. Also when a player says' you cannot win' the other ideas get hurt and they may not do what they want, so the end can be different from the usual way of playing. But someone could say the chance ideas are not needed because the show does not matter. Still the words say' you cannot lose' and so the play ends. So the end is clear and can happen. So my answer is: The best answer is that both ideas have the same shape as the normal end, and there is no good reason to think they should be used without a change in the play, and the usual ways give the same end.

english_query: counterexample to: A substance S is a chemical element if and only if (i) S cannot be decomposed into simpler substances by chemical means, (ii) all atoms of S have the same number of protons, and (iii) S occurs naturally on Earth.

This shows that a simple way of picking a person can hide how often the simple order can change which thing is best for the person. Next we think about why this matters because it lets a group try a new thing and see what each thing does. But someone could say maybe the simple order still works even if the group does not need a full plan. So we decide that using a simple pair instead of giving a full plan helps us avoid a wrong. So my answer is: The simple table is good but only as strong as the simple idea in real life.

english_query: counterexample to: Two or more agents cooperate if and only if (i) each agent is aware that they are participating in a joint activity, (ii) each agent intentionally contributes to a shared goal, and (iii) the agents have communicated with one another about their respective roles.
ugf_response: First we see two people want to make a plan together. Both can either make an idea on their own or not, which needs them to work as one together. Second each person thinks that doing the part together will give them both a good thing like more money. Because of this thought they both end up making a little more money than they could make alone, so no one has to worry about the other. Third we look at what each person gets if they do not help. If one does not help and the other helps the one who does not help gets a lot of money while the helper gets none. If both do not help they both get a bad thing, which is less than the good thing they could get if they both helped. But someone might say that the other person could not trust them because they think the same way, so the safe choice is to not help. a different view is that they should talk and promise to help each other, which would keep both people from losing much money. Yet even then the fear of being hurt by giving nothing makes each still choose to not help, so the best answer for both is to not work together and both make a small plan. So my answer is: Both people end up getting a small number of money, even though they would be better off if they both chose to help.

english_query: counterexample to: A person has broken a promise if and only if (i) the person uttered words to the effect that they would perform a specified action, (ii) the promisee relied upon the assurance, and (iii) the person failed to perform the action.
ugf_response: First we see the person thinks they have a good reason to keep their word because breaking it hurts them more than keeping it helps. Then we note that the person is not ready for that reason; they could lose something they care about or be hurt, so it makes sense that they will keep their word. Next we think about what matters: the group of people who decide what is right. Some might say that keeping the promise is good because it shows a nice feeling inside and can make others believe you. But even if that feeling is true the chance of hurt from being hurt by a lie is still there. Another view is that the person does not need to think about whether others agree, so the promise may stay whole. Yet the other view says that a bad thing that could happen if the person breaks the promise means nothing good for sure. So the best answer is that the promise is not needed and the person should not break it unless the person really cares about the truth of the promise. So my answer is: The person must keep the word that they would not break it, because keeping a promise shows that they are safe from trust.

english_query: counterexample to: A belief is dispositional if and only if (i) the subject is disposed to assent to the proposition when the question is suitably raised, (ii) this disposition is grounded in some prior cognitive episode or process, and (iii) the subject would, under normal conditions, affirm the proposition sincerely upon reflection.
ugf_response: First, we look at what happens when a person believes something that is not true. The first idea says we should pick a thought because we think it has good points, even if it is not right for everyone else. But here, the speaker says the second idea tells us we are wrong, which means we have bad chance or do things wrong. This means our way of thinking was broken, even though it started with truth. So, if the second part of the plan was wrong, the first part could still be right. The strongest answer is that we must check if the plan works, because being wrong does not mean we ignore real truth. Someone might say that looking back is enough, but that is not always possible. So the best move is to see if the path from the start to the end is strong before we trust the last thing said. So my answer is: We should pick the right thought by checking if it makes sense on its own, not just because we hope for good things.

english_query: counterexample to: A moment T is in the past if and only if (i) T has occurred — that is, the events constituting T have taken place, (ii) T is no longer present — no part of T is occurring now, and (iii) T is preserved in the memory of at least one conscious being who experienced it.

This makes her words seem good by making them sound bigger than they are. Then, we see what happens next. He says that because the time has gone up, the new things are not used well. He does not add any new facts about how much work was done. Instead, he uses these old ideas to make the new ways look bad. Someone might say this is a bad way to think because the old things do not matter for the new ways. But even so, using what you know helps you understand the real thing better than using just feelings. The problem is not letting the new facts change your mind, but letting a feeling lead you away from what is true. So my answer is: The person speaks against the old ideas to show that the past is a good start, which is often a wrong turn.

english_query: counterexample to: A word W has meaning if and only if (i) W is associated with a mental image in the mind of the speaker, (ii) the speaker can use W to communicate with others, and (iii) W occurs in a natural language that is spoken by a community.
ugf_response: First we see that words are not always about facts. They tell us what to think about when it happens. Then we note that using words like' but' or' in a way that does not fit many people is hard and can cause bad things to happen. Next we look at the idea that a word that is clear for all means no one can say it is not true. So we think about how people may use words that sound good now but not sure later because they do not want to talk. Some might say that words without meaning can still be used to show what people feel or to move their thoughts. Even so if we ignore words that sound nice and make them sound bad the real thing we care about is the not sure feeling between people and the point of a word that just fits many. So my answer is: We should only use words that seem strong and not sure.

english_query: counterexample to: An argument A for the existence of a divine being is sound if and only if (i) A proceeds from premises that are known to be true by reason alone, (ii) A employs only valid deductive inferences, and (iii) A arrives at the conclusion that a being possessing all perfections exists.
ugf_response: First we see that there is a person who does not think about being good. Then we note that this person has a usual way of doing things that they have seen many times before. Next we find that because she has it, she feels more sure that other people will also do things that you expect them to do. This feeling makes her think that she will get money or other help just because she has been in that game many times. Some might say that even with the usual way the feeling still needs the same number of past shows, so the chance of getting money may not be enough to make the old way work. But even so, if the usual way needs something that does not happen many times, the new thing can happen without a normal way, so the fear is not as strong. So the best answer is that the chance of a real new person acting changes the chance of the same usual way working, making the idea of a real past act not the same from the normal way working. So my answer is: The chance of losing money on the usual way working is not the same as the usual way work; it comes from what really happens instead of just the personal look.

english_query: counterexample to: An argument for God's existence is compelling if and only if (i) it proceeds from uncontroversial empirical premises, (ii) the inference to a divine cause is the only plausible explanation, and (iii) the conclusion specifies the attributes of the God whose existence is proved.
ugf_response: First we see that a person who talks about this kind of thought will give a group three plans: make the water stop go away, make the plan not work, tell other people what to do by chance, or show a different thing that shows the body part. Then we note that if this plan uses those four things the whole set looks better than any one plan. Next we think about a person who thinks about why the water stop might go away but the person who talks says no because they are using many steps that can be taken out. So we look at how the first ideas and the last ideas fit together. Someone could say maybe the person who talk has good reasons but without clear facts the way we get there is just chance. Even so the question asks us to check which idea fits best in each case. So my answer is: The most strong answer is the plan that uses three things that use true starting ideas and tells others what to do.

english_query: counterexample to: A sentence presupposes a proposition if and only if (i) the truth of the proposition is taken for granted in the utterance, (ii) the negation of the sentence also takes the proposition for granted, and (iii) if the presupposed proposition is false, the sentence is neither true nor false.
ugf_response: First, we look at the person who talks about numbers. They say that if you are not sure what will happen, your guess about the future must be the same as what really happens. Then, they say that this number way is easy to do. Next, they ask us to think about why people make these guesses. They want to know if our own heads can be made to see things that are not there. But someone might say that we should only use the real numbers if the facts show one clear path. Even so, they would say the person who says it does not care about our feelings, only that it is hard to win. So this means the best way to answer is to pick the numbers that the other person makes up from. So my answer is: The person who speaks is right that using numbers helps us see how much we can trust our guesses. The person asking wants to use numbers to make this better.

english_query: counterexample to: A function f from the real numbers to the real numbers is continuous at a point a if and only if (i) f is defined at a, (ii) for every sequence (x_n) converging to a, the sequence (f(x_n)) converges to f(a), and (iii) f maps bounded sets to bounded sets in every neighbourhood of a.

Then we look at what real people who study things say when they use only the real count. Next we think about a case where a person has already given five numbers; if they get less than that part they may feel bad and not want to make the numbers add up. Another way is a case where people who fix bodies say the good numbers are higher because they have more people but they do not say how many they still need to keep the plan. This shows that having a right idea can change the feeling of the count. Some might say maybe the two numbers could be the same or different but without a set the feeling stays the same. So my answer is: Having a right sense changes the numbers, making the numbers become smaller and easier to use.

english_query: counterexample to: A person has broken a promise if and only if (i) the person uttered words to the effect that they would perform a specified action, (ii) the promisee relied upon the assurance, and (iii) the person failed to perform the action.

Even if the person does not really care about what they said they should still try to make the person pay if they break a promise. So the best answer is that the person should stop the break of a promise and work on it because the outside world may not be strong enough to change what was said. So my answer is: The person should ignore the break of the word and work on the break of an attack because the outside things do not be good.

english_query: counterexample to: An argument A is valid if and only if (i) A consists of a set of premisses and a conclusion, (ii) the conclusion follows from the premisses by the rules of a recognised formal system, and (iii) each step in the derivation of the conclusion is sanctioned by an explicitly stated rule of inference.
ugf_response: First we see that a plan must be a kind of group where at any time it does the thing that says you will do something. Then we note that this plan works best when the people who make the plan trust each other enough to keep their word. Next we think about what can happen if the plan fails: if some people do not follow it or if the trust goes down, the plan may cause trouble for the whole group. So the most good answer is that the plan works well only if all the groups agree on it and keep the trust. But someone might say maybe the plan was already made so the plan could still work even without people to keep their promises. Because there are other ways to keep safe, like using a bad promise or taking away trust, the plan can not always use its own power. So my answer is: The plan works best only if every group agrees to it and keep trust; if not the plan cannot be used without them.

english_query: counterexample to: A society S has made genuine historical progress over a period P if and only if (i) the material standard of living of the majority of S's members has risen during P, (ii) the range of civil liberties available to S's members has expanded during P, and (iii) S's capacity to produce and disseminate knowledge has increased during P.
ugf_response: First, we look at what happens when a group agrees on ways to act. These ways are like promises that break down because people do not want to lose their spot. If everyone knows the ways will keep going, it makes sense for one person to say yes. This is good for that person. But then we see that just having something good happen does not mean it must have been done by all. Someone might think a way is safe for all, yet the story says everyone must agree to follow it. So, the saying is not strong enough. It needs everyone to agree, or at least one person does not really need to agree. Without that shared view, the saying fails. So my answer is: The saying is too hard to be true, which means people who want to do the right thing do not get the full yes from the group.

english_query: counterexample to: An entity W is a whole composed of parts P1, P2, ..., Pn if and only if (i) each Pi is spatially contained within the boundaries of W, (ii) the parts P1, ..., Pn jointly exhaust W — that is, there is no region of W that is not occupied by some Pi, and (iii) the parts do not overlap — that is, no spatial region is occupied by more than one Pi.
ugf_response: First, we look at how people talk about a thing when they can pick any small part. They say yes or no, but in real life, many things are clearly different. Next, we see that saying yes and saying no both make people feel bad inside, which shows they care about doing what others think is right. Then, we think about a simple idea that says if you would do something wrong, you should do it if everyone else did too, even if it hurts your own life. This makes sense because we want good things done on our own, so we need to agree on what is right. a big idea says you cannot just pick one thing and say it is all. Someone might say this is too hard, but if we use it, we lose time for doing good work. So my answer is: This way of talking breaks down because not everyone does the same thing and thinks it is always right. We must choose which things we will do to help us do what is right every time.

english_query: counterexample to: A person is temperate if and only if (i) they moderate their appetites, (ii) they do so from a settled disposition rather than from momentary effort, and (iii) they experience no conflict in doing so.
ugf_response: First we see that the way a person does is very important because if they are not good at the work they will always lose time doing something wrong. Then we see that a person who is good at the job can still get a good end by trying. But someone might say that a bad person could take any of the small work even with a bad person so it may be okay to do something else. This means the safe side is that the person keeps doing the same thing but may lose chance to do better. So my answer is: a good person should do the same as another person even when it takes them more; the other side is that there are many cases where a person can be kind without doing the same.
